data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Quantitative Tightening Continues
data.description:
The Federal Reserve lowered its policy rate of interest in the middle of September 2024, by 50 basis points.

It promised to continue to keep its policy of quantitative tightening going...although at a reduced rate...as it continued to move into the future.

So, this "new" period of quantitative tightening has begun and the Fed has kept its promise.

Thus, in the first phase of quantitative tightening, from March 16, 2022, to September 18, 2024, the Federal Reserve reduced its "securities held outright" by $1,804.4 billion or by $1.8 trillion.

Since September 18, 2024, the Federal Reserve has reduced its "securities held outright", by $44.1 billion.

Round 2 of the Fed's quantitative tightening has begun.

Since the end of the Great Recession and before the quantitative tightening began, the Federal Reserve conducted four rounds of quantitative easing.

Overall the "excess reserves" of the commercial banking system, otherwise labeled "Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks," have roughly remained constant.

Little else can be said at this time about the Fed's policy except that the Fed is continuing to act as it stated it would. Thus, the Fed continues to build trust in financial markets.

The one thing that has been happening that has been of some concern to the analysts is that the M2 money supply has remained relatively constant for some time.

The concern is that this will result in slower growth for the economy...or, even a recession.

But, as I have been arguing for some time now, we consider the buildup of bank liquidity to combat the recession, the banking system has plenty of "cash on hand."

Here is the picture of the M2 money stock growth since the first of 2020.

If one looks at the chart from a March 2022 date, one sees that there has been a decline in the growth of the M2 money stock.

However, if one begins early in 2020, the M2 money stock has actually grown by 27.3 percent from then up to the present date.

The compound annual rate of growth during this particular period of time is over 7.0 percent.

No one really questions the fact that a compound annual rate of growth in excess of 7 percent is a very expansive rate of increase. Inflationary even.

And, this is why the excess reserves in the commercial banking system are around $3.3 trillion.

But, the other factor in this equation is that the velocity of circulation of the M2 money stock declined substantially during this period of time. Money was pumped into the banking system, but the money did not enter the "real" circuit of the economy...the money stayed in the "financial" circuit.

Thus, the "cash" is there...in the banking system...but the "cash" has not been used to purchase goods and services.

Thus, the Federal Reserve has seen inflationary pressures build up as the M2 money stock increased, but it did not see a sustained period of inflation because the money did not get into the spending circuit.

This is one of the things that the Federal Reserve is now fighting with its second round of quantitative tightening.

The Federal Reserve still has a ways to go to get the banking system back to a place where there are not lots and lots of cash lying around the bank vaults.

The Federal Reserve must still remove securities from the banking system so that all the "excess" cash is removed and "normal" business can move along without the fear that the "excess" cash hanging around will turn into another inflationary threat.

The Federal Reserve has done a real good job up to the present time.

Let's hope that it can continue to finish the job so that the banking system can return to a more normal position.

Here we see the Fed account "Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve Banks," something I refer to as "excess reserves."

You can see here the rise in these "excess reserves" as the Federal Reserve added, aggressively, securities to its securities portfolio.

Obviously, this figure shot up as the 2020 recession took place, and then even increased further as the economy moved into 2021 and 2022.

With the beginning of quantitative tightening, these reserve balances began to decline.

As can be seen in the latter half of 2023 these reserve balances increased somewhat as the Federal Reserve responded to the bank failures that took place during this time.

But, these reserve balances have not been trending downward once again up to the present time.

Readers should keep a close watch on this series going forward.

The main policy tool of the Federal Reserve is what it is doing to its holdings of securities.

The series on reserve balances should be watched to see how this series moves with any Federal Reserve efforts to manage its policy rate of interest.

This is the second part of the Fed's policy approach.

Yes, the Fed is in a stage of quantitative tightening.

But, the Fed has lowered its policy rate of interest and has indicated that, in the future, it would be looking at whether or not it should reduce its policy rate of interest further.

Here we need to watch what is happening to the reserve balances account, the "excess reserves" account.

Here we need to be aware of the pressure commercial banks are feeling on the excess cash they are carrying on their balance sheets.

Listen to what the Fed is saying, but also watch what the Fed is causing to happen.

Given the state of the U.S. economy, Federal Reserve officials may not be as anxious to further lower the Fed's policy rate of interest as it has talked about in recent weeks.

The Fed's policy rate of interest may stay higher for a longer period of time than market participants might think.

Stay tuned.

data.title: Motorcar Parts of America On A Bumpy Ride, Investors May Want To Steer Clear
data.description:
Thesis

Motorcar Parts of America (NASDAQ:MPAA) has really tried. They've been busy expanding their product range and sharpening up their operations, no doubt. But, when you step back and take in the full panorama, the company's long-standing financial woes and dismal stock performance rather leap out at you like a poorly timed airbag deployment. Yes, sales are up, and they've cut some costs, but with the ongoing issues in earnings growth, market sentiment, and industry pressure, I think it makes sense for investors to explore more stable options elsewhere.

Automotive Aftermarket Specialist

Motorcar Parts of America, Inc., is a leading automotive aftermarket provider that remanufactures and markets various kinds of replacement parts. It began in 1968, when Mel Marks founded the company to primarily import European and Japanese parts. But MPAA soon mushroomed in scope and products. They supply everything from alternators and starters to brakes and other important automotive parts. They are well established in domestic and foreign markets and supply large retail chains and repair locations in the United States, Canada, and Mexico.

Over the years, the company has spread itself across the globe, popping up with manufacturing and administrative hubs in China, and Malaysia. Its subsidiaries, such as Dixie Electric and D&V Electronics, expand the company’s reach into the heavy-duty truck sector, aerospace, and even electric motor testing.

Stock Performance Concerns

Despite its global expansion and diversified product offerings, the financial performance tells a different story. The numbers don't look good for MPAA. The stock has taken a huge hit, especially when compared to the S&P 500 (SPY). From March 2016 to October 2024, MPAA’s performance should raise some serious red flags for investors.

Back in 2016, MPAA was priced at $37.34. Now, it's down to $6.05—a brutal drop of over 83%. An investor who put $10,000 into MPAA in 2016 would now have just $1,620. Meanwhile, that same $10,000 in the S&P 500 would’ve grown to over $28,000, or even $30,400 with dividends.

Speaking of dividends, MPAA doesn’t offer any. The S&P 500, on the other hand, paid out $2,260 in dividends over this period. Dividends usually help soften the blow when stock prices dip. Without that safety net, MPAA stockholders are feeling the full impact of its decline. Investors typically look for either price growth or steady income from dividends. MPAA offers neither. The company’s negative compound growth rate of -19.18% compared to the S&P’s 13.90% just drives my point home.

Financial Results

Despite the troubling stock performance and lack of dividends, MPAA insists there are some bright spots in their recent financial report. According to their latest results, the company highlights a decent product mix and some growth in key areas. They break down their revenue like this: 65% comes from rotating electrical products, 24% from brakes, 7% from wheel hubs, and 4% from other products. While this shows some level of diversification, the heavy dependence on rotating electrical products raises questions for me about how balanced and resilient this mix really is.

Record Sales Performance

Despite my concerns about diversification, there are, it seems, a few rays of sunshine poking through MPAA’s recent financials: Q1 results for 2025 tell us that the company managed to pull off a 6.4% rise in net sales, hitting $169.9 million, along with a 9.8% jump in gross profit, bringing it to $29.2 million. This little uptick, we’re told, comes partly from the company’s efforts to streamline operations, with a tidy $7 million in annual savings expected from severance and some good old-fashioned cost-cutting. Adding to the good news, July 2025 saw the highest sales month in the company’s history for that period, pointing to growing demand and potentially setting the stage for future growth.

Growth Potential

While these factors might look promising at first glance, I think it's worth asking how much of this is just speculation, especially with the uncertainty around interest rates and demand recovery. Likewise, the strong performance of the brake program might not be enough to balance out the company's bigger financial issues. Customer satisfaction with the Quality-Built brand has been solid, particularly in brake pads and rotors, but it’s unclear if this loyalty will drive sustainable growth across other product lines.

Meanwhile, MPAA has introduced over 150 new part numbers, covering an additional 49 million vehicles, which, according to management, should help fuel some organic growth. They’ve also ventured into the heavy-duty market—agriculture, Class 8 trucks, construction—which is seen as another promising step. Still, there are real concerns about whether MPAA can keep up the momentum.

They’re forecasting sales growth for fiscal 2025 in the range of 3.9% to 6.6%, aiming for revenue between $746 million and $766 million. But, given their less-than-stellar history, I think it's fair for an investor to ask: are these targets realistic or just a tad too optimistic?

Expanding Into New Markets

Alongside these positive moves, one of MPAA’s more exciting growth opportunities is its expansion into Mexico. Management views the rising average vehicle age and increased disposable income in the region as key drivers of a potential $100 million market.

While this fits into MPAA’s broader strategy to tap into long-term industry growth, it’s also fair to question if the aging U.S. vehicle population—now averaging 12.6 years—is enough to sustain these goals. Similarly, the new wheel hub manufacturing facility in Malaysia, while promoted as boosting MPAA’s competitive edge, raises doubts about how much it will really help with the company’s broader challenges.

Growth in Diagnostic Business

Adding to its geographic and product expansion, MPAA’s diagnostic business is picking up steam, especially as major chains start using its products, which creates a nice high-margin revenue stream. Their heavy-duty rotating electrical business is also growing, giving more depth to their expansion plans. The company points to over $90 million in liquidity as proof that it's in a good spot to take advantage of these growth opportunities.

Stock Valuation and Concerns

If you were to ignore all the challenges and looming risks, it’s worth pointing out that, at $6.05 per share, the stock seems a bit undervalued when you look at its fundamentals and market position. The P/E ratio is sitting at 12.29x, just shy of the usual 12.40x, so the stock is pretty much priced in line with historical averages.

What’s particularly troubling, at least to me, is the company's earnings growth—or rather, the lack thereof: a -7.94% drop in operating earnings suggests that MPAA is struggling to keep its head above water in terms of profitability. In an industry that’s notoriously cyclical and prone to the whims of broader economic shifts, this isn’t just a small hiccup; it’s a serious red flag. The P/E ratio doesn’t quite square with this earnings performance either, which hints that the market may be a little too optimistic about a turnaround, or perhaps just blissfully unaware of the company’s deeper issues.

When it comes to valuation, the fair value ratio of 15.00x shows the stock is priced below the industry average and that could mean some upside potential if MPAA manages to fix its operational problems, but with no clear signs of an earnings recovery, it’s tough to get too excited. The 8.14% EPS yield looks solid, though the lack of a dividend takes away some of the shine. On the bright side, the debt-to-capital ratio of 14.70% isn’t anything to worry about.

Industry Pressures Persist

Despite some of the earlier bullish points, MPAA’s first quarter came with its fair share of challenges. The company reported a sharp net loss of $18.1 million, largely driven by an $11.1 million non-cash foreign exchange loss, thanks to the U.S. dollar’s strength against the Mexican peso. This was a significant drop from the $1.4 million loss they posted the previous year.

Adding to the struggle was a dip in retail inventory demand, with consumers putting off vehicle maintenance due to economic pressures, which could slow down short-term sales growth if consumer confidence stays low. On top of that, higher interest expenses, rising from $11.7 million to $14.4 million, further dragged down performance, leading to a negative EBITDA of $1.1 million.

Challenges in Brake Segment

While these financial setbacks certainly highlight some immediate challenges, MPAA’s growth prospects are not entirely without hope. There’s still potential, particularly in expanding key product lines like brakes, wheel hubs, and diagnostic tools. The brake segment, in particular, is expected to grow nicely by January 2025—at least, that’s the plan. But sluggish demand in the tire industry is hurting this outlook. When consumers put off tire replacements, MPAA loses key cross-selling chances for brakes, slowing growth. On top of that, progress in their electric vehicle (EV) testing business has been slower than expected, resulting in weaker performance than the company had hoped for.

Looking ahead, MPAA seems to have a few things going for it: with diverse revenue streams and decent performance across key product lines, there’s potential for growth in the coming quarters. This all sounds promising, of course, and could very well set the company up for future success, but, as always, there are a few clouds on the horizon: weak retail inventory demand and the ever-present economic uncertainty are still looming risks. When MPAA reports its Q2 2025 earnings on November 8, 2024, after the bell, we’ll get a better sense of how these factors are shaping the company’s near-term outlook.

Analyst Estimates and Business Uncertainty

If you’re looking to Wall Street for insights, it’s clear analysts have been a bit too optimistic. This ongoing pattern of overestimation could mean a few things. For one, it suggests that MPAA’s business has become more unpredictable than expected. The sharp decline in actual earnings—dropping from $1.87 in 2021 to $0.41 in 2024—shows that MPAA may be facing some deeper structural issues.

For you, as an investor, this is a cautionary tale. Analyst coverage, which was a decent five in 2017, has now plummeted to just one in 2024. A declining number of analysts typically signals that the stock is losing momentum or a certain degree of confidence, which sometimes could cause less liquidity (waning interest) and, hence, more volatility. This sharp decline in earnings — and the increasing divergence between estimate and reality — suggests that MPAA might not only have lost its growth narrative but also maybe even its ability to maintain earnings. Given these trends, it’s probably wise for investors to take those forward estimates with a hefty pinch of salt.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I would rate MPAA a "Sell." Keep in mind, if you look at my reports, I’m not one to readily assign a "sell" rating, but considering that the stock has significantly underperformed over the long term, dropping over 83% since 2016, with no dividends to cushion losses, I’ll be blunt: why wait this out when there are better opportunities out there for your money? While there are some positive developments in the company's operations, such as rising sales and cost-saving initiatives, the consistent earnings decline, negative market sentiment, and broader industry challenges just make this stock too risky and too much of a distraction without clear signs of sustained recovery.

data.title: Evolution Petroleum: An Investment Opportunity For Dividend-Oriented Investors
data.description:
In this article, I’m going to provide an investment recommendation for Evolution Petroleum Corporation (NYSE:EPM), an oil and gas company focused on the US onshore natural gas. I had already written a report about Evolution Petroleum about one year ago, in which I was suggesting a buy recommendation: despite the stock price dropping by 22% since that, I still believe Evolution is a company worth investing in.

In the following paragraphs, I will provide an overview of the Fully Year 2024 financial results and I will explain why I believe the company is a BUY.

New assets added during FY2024

When I wrote my last article, I mentioned that Evolution Petroleum had stakes in five different assets: Delhi Field, Hamilton Dome Field, Jonah Field, Williston Basin and Barnett Shale. In January 2024, the company carried out an acquisition of non-operated assets, the SCOOP and STACK plays in Oklahoma Central, for a consideration of 43.5 M$. The acquisition adds 1.5 kboe/d produced via 230 producing wells with a production mix composed by 42% of oil, 43% of natural gas and the remaining 15% of NGLs.

In September 2023, Evolution Petroleum signed a definitive Participation Agreement with PEDEVCO for the joint development of the Chaveroo oilfield in the Northwest Shelf of southeastern New Mexico. Subject to the Participation Agreement is a zone of 16,000 acres where PEDEVCO has a 100 WI. According to the Agreement, Evolution Petroleum will farm-in with a 50% WI share in the future horizontal drilling locations on a block-by-block basis, with PEDEVCO acting as operator.

Evolution Petroleum production and reserves

Looking at the production during FY2024, oil production increased by 8% year-on-year to 709 kbbls while natural gas and NGLs production both decreased by 10% and 3% to 8,244 MMcf and 402 kbbls, respectively. Overall, the annual production expressed in thousands of barrels of oil equivalent was 2,485 (or 6.8 kboe/d) down 4% y-o-y.

Despite the annual production, FY2024 proved reserves are quite in line with FY2023 due to the new contribution of SCOOP/STACK and Chaveroo Field: at the end of FY2024, proved reserves amounted to 31.7 Mboe. In terms of product mix, Evolution Petroleum proved reserves are well-balanced with 37% being represented by oil, 41% by natural gas and the remaining 22% by NGLs: from my point of view, the diversification of the production portfolio is a strength that plays in favor of Evolution Petroleum since it gives the company flexibility and resilience since it is not too much dependent on oil prices or natural gas prices.

From a field point of view, most of the proved reserves are in the Barnett Shale (25%), in the Willinston Basin (18%) and in the Johan Field (15%).

Full Year 2024 – Financial results

During 2024, Evolution Petroleum generated revenues for $86 million, down 33% year-on-year ($128 million in FY2023). The reason behind this drop can be found in the overall reduction in production, -4% y-o-y, but above all in the reduction of the average realized price, $34.6/boe, down 30% y-o-y. The decline is not homogenous across all the products: indeed, revenues from oil increased by 5% to $53 million while revenues from natural gas and NGLs declined by 66% and 20% respectively. The strong drop in natural gas revenues is explained both by the reduction in production (-10% y-o-y) and, more importantly, by the decline in the average realized selling price, from 7 $/Mcf in FY2023 to $2.6/Mcf in FY2024 (-63%). A similar trend characterized the NGLs revenues, with volumes declining by 3% y-o-y and the average price by 17%.

Looking at costs, total operating expenses (OpEx) declined by 7% driven by a 19% reduction in lease operating cost (from $60 million in FY2023 to $48 million in FY2024) mostly due to a -$6 million in the Barnett Shale and a -$4 in the Delhi Field. D&A increase by 40% y-o-y while G&A remained constant.

Consequently, EBIT was $7.9 million, down 82% y-o-y while net income was $4 million, down 88% y-o-y.

Cash flows, liquidity and debt

Cash flows from operations was for FY2024 was $23 million, due to the generated net income and the depreciation being added back. Cash flows from investing were -$49 million due to the acquisition mentioned in the previous paragraph as well as some other acreage that was added across different fields. Cash flows provided by financing activities were positive at $22 million, with the most relevant movements being the $16 million paid out as dividends and the $42.5 million drawdown under a senior secured credit facility used to finance the SCOOP/STACK acquisition.

At the end of FY2024, the total cash stands at $6.4 million, and, with a $39.5 million debt, the net debt is -$33.1 million.

Valuation

In order to understand how Evolution Petroleum is trading compared to the market, I looked at multiples for some of Evolution Petroleum peers. In particular, I selected SM Energy Company, Matador Resources Company and Permian Resources Corporation. Looking at the EV/EBITDA, one can notice that compared Evolution Petroleum is trading at higher values than peers (5.5x vs 3.7x average). However, the higher EV/EBITDA is justified by a Dividend Yield that is almost 9%, well above the 2.9% average of peers. In other words, Evolution Petroleum is currently more expensive than its peers but, at the same time, it is providing more value through dividends: since 2013, the company has always used dividends as a way to remunerate its shareholders with $114 million paid out overall since 2013.

Risks

From my point of view, Evolution Petroleum is mostly exposed to the risk associated with the oil and gas price fluctuation. As analyzed in the previous paragraph, revenues are driven by two main factors: produced volumes and price. While production can be more easily planned and managed, price is determined by global dynamics that are out of Evolution Petroleum hands. However, Evolution Petroleum management is well aware of this risk and may decide to open a hedging position to mitigate the price fluctuations.

Why Evolution Petroleum is a BUY

Overall, I believe that Evolution Petroleum is a good investment opportunity. From a financial health perspective, the company is sound and has a strong ability to manage costs, as shown by the 7% yearly reduction, and to maintain profitability despite a FY2024 with lower oil and gas prices than in FY2023. The net debt ($33 million) is what I would call “good net debt” since it was mostly generated by the acquisition of the SCOOP/STACK fields that will offer a growth opportunity in the next quarters.

I suggest the stock to investors who are looking for dividends and prudent financial management rather than extraordinary stock price growth.

data.title: GTO: Actively Managed Bond Fund From Invesco
data.description:
Thesis

The Invesco Total Return Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:GTO) is an actively managed fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name targets the intermediate portion of the yield curve, and has a very wide mandate. As per the Seeking Alpha landing page, the fund:

Invests in fixed income markets of global region. It invests directly and through other funds in corporate bonds, treasury securities, collateralized loan obligations, bank loans or corporate loans, mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities issued by various U.S. and non-U.S. public- or private-sector entities, and municipal securities issued by states or local governments and their agencies, authorities and other government sponsored enterprises with varying maturities and credit qualities.

Given the recent spike in yields on the back of better than expected economic data, the intermediate portion of the yield curve yet again looks appealing, especially for actively managed funds. We like active management in today's environment because it provides for the flexibility needed to take advantage of non-linear opportunities.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at GTO, its composition and holdings, as well as articulate why we think the name is appealing at the current levels.

Wide mandate, focus on IG holdings

The ETF has a very wide mandate, but when parsing out its ratings we can see it is actually focused on investment grade ('IG') bonds:

  • AAA names: 38.4%
  • AA names: 10.5%
  • A names: 12%
  • BBB names: 17%
  • BB names: 6%
  • Not Rated: 16%
  • Other: residual

AAA names make up almost 40% of the holdings, and in fact very highly rated bonds (i.e. AAA + AA + A) are almost 61% of the fund. This indicates a name which will be largely affected by rates rather than credit spreads.

When it comes to fund composition, we can see that Agency securities are the largest holding in this ETF:

In its parsing on the website, Invesco seems to co-mingle the security type with sectoral allocations. In the above table for example 'Financials' represents corporate bonds that come from companies that fall in the 'Financials' sector. Agency securities on the other hand denote the type of bonds. There is a better parsing in the fund fact sheet, but please note this is from June 2024:

The best way to think about this name is as a 33 / 33 / 33 allocation among Treasuries, Agency MBS bonds and Corporates. This allocation changes all the time as the portfolio managers see fit. As an example, please note that in June 2024 the AAA bucket for the fund was 53% (i.e. Treasuries + Agency MBS), but this figure has now come down to 38% in the span of a few months. Expect this dynamic to persist.

Yield and duration considerations

This ETF targets the intermediate portion of the yield curve via its duration profile:

The fund has a 6.5 years modified duration, and its portfolio has a 5.75% yield to maturity. Kindly note the low figure is due to the fact that the ETF is overweight highly rated securities, with rates the main risk driver here.

GTO represents an active way to take advantage of the current elevated yields in the intermediate portion of the curve, without layering in credit spread risk.

Performance has been impressive

GTO falls in an active funds cohort, and we have covered a number of exchange-traded funds in this category before:

  • JPIE: An Attractive Way To Take Advantage Of Fed Cuts
  • PYLD: CMOs Are Still Attractive, Reiterate Buy
  • BOND: Peak Rates Suggest Positive Performance

Let us have a look at how GTO's total return benchmarks against its competition:

PYLD is the outperformer here, followed by GTO and then BOND when looking at the performance in the last year. Kindly keep in mind that the above funds have different duration profiles, with the longest ones being able to offer the best returns when rates move significantly lower. What these funds have in common is the active management, ability to invest in different asset types and rating bands. With a 13.2% total return in the past year, GTO has clearly proven its mettle, considering it has provided returns in excess of certain equity indices.

What is next for GTO

We feel that actively managed funds are going to be able to deliver total returns well in excess of their yields to maturity. In a theoretical example where intermediate rates move down by 100 bps, the fund will be able to generate a capital gain of 6.5% from its duration component, plus a sizable dividend yield, thus providing for a 10% plus total return.

While there is a lot of volatility currently in the rates sector, the direction is down for rates as the Fed cuts further. When in a monetary easing cycle, investors should buy bonds and duration. Unlike their corporate IG or HY peers, funds like GTO focus on highly rated names, which are generally driven by rates rather than credit spreads. This translates into names like GTO doing well even during a recession, since rates move lower aggressively in a recession, compensating for the widening in spreads.

GTO is in a unique position where it can benefit from both a soft landing and a recession. The only risk scenario for this name is the re-emergence of an inflationary pulse, which would force the Fed to raise rates again. We are of the opinion that such a scenario is very remote, with the Fed likely to pause its rates cutting cycle and not hike again.

Conclusion

GTO is a fixed income ETF from Invesco. The name is an actively managed bond fund that targets the intermediate portion of the yield curve. The ETF has a wide mandate, but it is mainly made up of treasuries, Agency MBS bonds and corporate IG bonds. The manager is very active in shuffling the allocations, and has managed a remarkable 13.2% total return in the past year. We are of the opinion that today's macro environment is ideal for actively managed bond funds, and we like the fact that rates are the main risk factor here. We are a buy for GTO here, targeting a 10% plus total return in the next 12 months.

data.title: Allied Properties REIT: Navigating Office Market Uncertainty
data.description:
All figures are in $CAD unless otherwise noted.

All financial information is from Seeking Alpha unless otherwise noted.

Our Take

Allied Properties REIT (TSX:AP.UN:CA)(OTC:APYRF) has several strong points, including a high-quality tenant base, leadership in the Canadian commercial office market, and a substantial discount to its book value. However, there are notable risks, such as an elevated payout ratio, the potential for a dividend cut, and the lofty valuations. The uncertain future of office space demand also adds to the challenge. While these risks are significant, Allied could still present an attractive opportunity for investors with a higher risk tolerance and long-term outlook. For others, we recommend a more cautious approach and waiting to see how their strategy evolves.

Introduction & Portfolio Overview

Allied Properties REIT is the largest office-focused real estate investment trust in Canada, overseeing 192 properties with a total valuation of $8.8 billion as of Q2 2024 (this figure doesn't include development properties or properties held for sale). The REIT's portfolio spans 14.9 million square feet of gross leasable area (GLA), with the majority concentrated in major urban centers like Montreal and Ottawa (43%) and Toronto and Kitchener (41%). The remaining 16% is split between Calgary and Edmonton (8%) and Vancouver (8%).

Allied is clearly concentrated in major Canadian urban markets, a logical strategy given that 82% of its net operating income comes from office space and an additional 6% from parking. The remaining 12% is derived from retail properties. As of the end of Q2 2024, Allied reported an occupancy rate of 87.1%, with a weighted average lease term of 5.8 years. The in-place rental rate was $25.08 per square foot, reflecting a 6.7% year-over-year increase.

The Portfolio Quality & Tenant Roster is Superior

Allied’s portfolio is primarily composed of 'AA' Downtown Office properties, positioning their assets as superior in both quality and location compared to competitors. This is a significant advantage, as these trophy assets tend to attract high-quality tenants, command higher rents, and experience stronger demand.

A review of the Q3 2024 CBRE Canada Office Figures Report further supports Allied's position. The report highlights a clear "flight to quality," with trophy assets recording the lowest vacancy rates in nearly four years, something we believe is a positive indicator for Allied’s premium office properties.

The figure above shows that trophy assets have a vacancy rate of 10.1%, which aligns with Allied Properties' slightly higher vacancy rate of 12.9%. This is consistent with our assessment that most of Allied’s portfolio is of a premium quality. Another item to note and something we consider important is that more than half of Allied's vacancies are concentrated in just 12 of their 192 properties, reinforcing the strength of their core assets.

Another key takeaway from the report is that office construction activity has reached a 20-year low, with declines over the past nine consecutive quarters. With new supply not entering the market, we expect the office market to stabilize over the coming years and this "flight to quality" should position Allied as a standout as there will be limited new 'trophy' assets coming online.

Allied's top tenants include industry giants such as Google (with a weighted average lease term of 8.1 years), Ubisoft (8 years), Morgan Stanley (5.4 years), and Shopify (10 years). These household names highlight the superior caliber of Allied’s properties and the strength of their tenant roster.

Diversification is also evident in Allied’s tenant base, with only about 20% of rent coming from their top 10 tenants. While many of these tenants are concentrated in business services, telecommunications, and media, there is a meaningful presence from other sectors such as finance, government, and retail, which adds a further layer of stability.

Allied’s lease retention rate of 66.8% in the first two quarters of 2024 is something we consider average. However, achieving this retention while also increasing rental spreads by 7.4% is a positive sign, though we would like to see more consistency in these metrics. There should be an opportunity for consistent improvement in 2025, as 10.6% of gross leasable area will come up for renewal, with an in-place rental rate of $23.96 per square foot versus an estimated market rate of $25.21 per square foot.

With a weighted average lease term to maturity of 5.8 years, we remain optimistic about Allied’s future, particularly in terms of portfolio and tenant quality. Allied is well-positioned to benefit from the stabilization of commercial office use in Canada, thanks to the superior quality of its assets, favorable vacancy trends, and the slowdown in new construction. The portfolio's strong tenant diversification and the high caliber of tenants provide an additional layer of support. While we expect positive leasing metrics to persist, they are key metrics that should be closely monitored.

The Apparent Undervaluation & Appealing Entry Point

Historically, Allied Properties has traded at a three-year average price-to-book (P/B) ratio of 0.619x. Currently, the REIT is trading at a P/B ratio of 0.478x. To put this into context, using the current book value per share of $43.14, Allied should be trading around $26.70 per share (calculated as $43.14 * 0.619), indicating a potential discount of approximately 30%.

If the worst of the office market downturn is behind us, this could offer investors an attractive entry point, especially with Allied Properties trading at one of the lowest price-to-book ratios in its history. However, this discount is not without reason. We believe there are significant risks tied to Allied, the broader Canadian economy, and the office market itself.

Additionally, we find the $9.83 billion in assets attributed to investment properties and properties held for sale (including $1.06 billion in properties under development) to be somewhat overvalued on the balance sheet.

The figure above shows the weighted average capitalization rates that Allied Properties uses to value its properties across different regions. By referencing the Q2 2024 CBRE Cap Rate Survey, we can compare the cap rates for 'AA' rated Downtown Office buildings to see how Allied's valuation aligns with the broader market. The table below provides our estimates of the cap rates we believe would apply to buildings of similar quality in Allied’s portfolio.

Location CBRE Cap Rate Range ('AA' Downtown Office) Our Estimate
Montreal 6.25% - 6.75% 5.75%
Ottawa 6.25% - 6.50% 5.75%
Toronto 5.25% - 6.00% 5.00%
Kitchener 6.00% - 6.75% 5.50%
Calgary 9.00% - 9.50% 8.00%
Vancouver 5.25% - 6.00% 5.00%

When comparing our estimates to the market range, we believe that the cap rates used on Allied’s balance sheet should be increased across all regions. For instance, Montreal and Ottawa properties are currently valued with a cap rate of 5.07%, whereas we estimate it to be closer to 5.75%.

As a result, we believe adding 50 basis points to the weighted average capitalization rate (bringing it to 5.32%) would provide a more conservative and potentially more accurate valuation. According to Allied’s 2024 Q2 Audited financial statements, a 50 bps increase would reduce the value of their properties by approximately $454 million, lowering the book value from $43.14 per share to around $39.89 (author's calculation: subtracting $454 million from total assets, subtracting liabilities, and dividing by shares outstanding). This adjustment would bring our price-to-book estimate down to $24.69 per share, representing a roughly 20% discount when we use the same methodology as we did at the start of this section.

In conclusion, we believe that the current undervaluation, when compared to historical norms, is justified given the heightened risk perception surrounding office properties. A closer look at the book value suggests it may be somewhat aggressive, and using a more conservative approach presents a less compelling undervaluation story. However, for investors with a long-term horizon and high risk tolerance, there remains significant upside if market sentiment around the office sector improves. Given the current backdrop of a volatile Canadian economy and negative market sentiment, we recommend staying on the sidelines for now.

Dividend Concerns

At the time of writing, Allied offers a dividend yield of 8.75%, paid monthly, which annualizes to about 9.09% due to the effects of compounding. Even for Canadian companies and REITs, which are known for higher dividend yields compared to their American counterparts, this yield would be considered high.

Since 2020, Allied's dividend yield has consistently risen: it averaged 3.89% in 2020, 3.97% in 2021, 5.05% in 2022, 8.23% in 2023, and has averaged 12.94% so far in 2024. This increase has been driven by both Allied’s gradual dividend growth (2.51% compounded annually over the last five years as per Seeking Alpha) and the sharp ~60% drop in its stock price since January 2020. On the surface, this might seem like an attractive yield for dividend-focused investors, especially for retirees who rely on dividend income. Allied’s yield is also appealing due to the diversification it offers, as most high-yield dividend stocks in Canada are concentrated in sectors like financials, utilities, and energy. However, we advise dividend-focused investors to be cautious for two key reasons:

  1. Limited Dividend Growth: The dividend’s growth has not kept pace with inflation, leading to eroding purchasing power.
  2. Dividend Risk: The sustainability of the dividend is questionable.

For REITs, we typically assess dividend safety using the adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) payout ratio rather than the traditional payout ratio, as net income for REITs can be volatile due to non-cash changes in property values. The AFFO payout ratio provides a clearer picture of the actual cash flow available to fund dividends.

In 2023, Allied's AFFO payout ratio was 82.7%, which we would consider high but relatively safe. However, in 2024, this ratio has increased to 88.8%, and for Q2 2024, it stood at 94.4%. A payout ratio exceeding 100% is unsustainable without resorting to debt issuance to cover dividend payments. While the future earnings visibility is clouded by several factors, we believe rising interest expenses will be a key drag on Allied’s financials going forward.

The figure above highlights that Allied Properties' interest expense in 2024 has risen by 8.1% compared to the same period in 2023. As the market adjusts to the post-pandemic era, marked by an increase in work-from-home arrangements, lenders have grown more cautious toward office properties, viewing them as higher risk (and thus requiring a higher return). In addition, Allied's current weighted average interest rate on debt is relatively low at 3.45%. However, with approximately $1.83 billion, or 42.7%, of their debt maturing by 2027, we anticipate an increase in their weighted average interest rate. As a result, we expect Allied to face higher interest expenses going forward. On the bright side, we do anticipate rental growth, which could help offset some of these rising costs. Nonetheless, we expect the AFFO payout ratio to remain close to 100% in the near future.

Given the many variables affecting Allied’s earnings visibility, there are risks that could push the payout ratio above 100%, potentially forcing management to cut the dividend. A significant negative shift in the office market, for instance, could have a considerable impact on Allied’s financials. However, it’s worth noting that management has expressed confidence in the sustainability of the dividend and remains committed to maintaining the distribution.

Assuming the dividend remains secure, the next critical question is, will the dividend grow? For retirees relying on dividend income, inflation and its impact on purchasing power must be considered. Using the Bank of Canada's inflation calculator, a basket of goods that cost $100 in 2019 now costs $118.28, reflecting an 18.28% increase over the past five years. In comparison, Allied Properties' dividend has only grown by 13.19% during the same period, meaning the dividend income has not kept pace with inflation, leading to a reduction in purchasing power.

With the AFFO payout ratio expected to hover near 100%, we believe that Allied will have limited ability to increase its dividend over the coming years. If this scenario plays out, investors relying on Allied’s distributions must account for the inflationary erosion of their purchasing power and its impact on maintaining their current lifestyle.

In summary, while we expect the AFFO payout ratio to stay close to 100%, we believe there is more downside risk to the dividend than upside. We do not anticipate significant dividend growth in the foreseeable future, which means purchasing power will likely continue to erode in an inflationary environment. Therefore, retirees and income-focused investors should be cautious about investing in Allied solely for its dividend yield, as the full picture requires a deeper understanding of the risks involved.

Other Quick Commentary

We feel a brief note on other items is warranted, which we will explore further in our next article. While Allied has significant potential with several projects currently in development, there are also risks if these projects do not meet expectations. Additionally, Allied faces the challenge of deleveraging its business, especially as lenders continue to tighten their criteria for office property loans. These factors represent ongoing risks for the REIT but could also lead to positive outcomes if managed well. With limited visibility on these developments at this time, we will provide more detailed commentary as updates become available.

Final Conclusion

Allied Properties REIT offers an intriguing investment opportunity for those with a long-term horizon and higher risk tolerance, primarily due to its superior portfolio quality, tenant roster, and significant undervaluation relative to book value. However, the challenges it faces, including increasing interest expenses, an elevated payout ratio, and uncertainty in the office market, are risks that cannot be overlooked. While there is potential upside if market sentiment improves, the current environment suggests caution. Investors focused on income should particularly be aware of the sustainability and growth prospects of the dividend, as it may not keep pace with inflation. For most, a wait-and-see approach is advised as Allied works to stabilize its business and reduce its risk exposure.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Dividend Capture Is A Tough Gig
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company ("BDC") sector from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of October.

Market Action

BDCs were down on the week, with underperformers PSEC and TPVG leading to the downside. The PSEC rally in September was a dead cat bounce and we continue to avoid the stock. We hold three of the four top performers this week such as FDUS, ARCC and NCDL which was good to see. Month-to-date the sector is fairly flat.

The average valuation in our coverage has been steady over the last few weeks.

The market has recently shaved off a whole cut off the Fed's expected trajectory for the rest of the year. This will support BDC net investment income through the rest of the year.

Market Themes

The Gladstone Investment Corp (GAIN) 4-Oct ex-dividend date came and went. The special dividend was unusually large at over 5%. It seems that there was quite a bit of dividend capture going on, which explains the sharp rally of the stock into the ex-div date late last week and its more recent underperformance.

Our view has always been that, rather than play the dividend capture game, it is more lucrative to play the dividend capture anticipation game, i.e. pre-position even earlier in the stock (before the dividend capture players do a week or two before the ex-div date) and then sell right before the ex-div date rather than after the ex-div date.

The idea here is not to capture the dividend but to capture the dynamics of dividend capture investors, i.e. sell the shovels to gold diggers, rather than dig for gold. This would have worked very well again this time around. In the month leading up to the ex-div date, the stock rallied by over 18%.

The reason we know the dividend capture didn’t work is that the stock opened down $0.74 on the ex-div date, i.e. more than offsetting the dividend. The price then continued to fall and moved another $0.5 lower on Monday.

The other thing to keep in mind is that the current valuation of GAIN looks better than it really is. We need to subtract the $0.7 dividend from the NAV which gives us $12.30 and which results in a valuation of 109% and not the 103% based on the Q3 NAV.

Market Commentary

Saratoga Investment (SAR) reported earnings. Because of its earlier calendar, it serves as a canary in the coalmine for the full earnings reporting season.

Non-accruals fell to 0.3% on fair-value as previous problem assets were resolved. The NAV rose by 1%, primarily due to retained income. Net realized/unrealized losses were about 1.3%. This past year was the worst for the company in terms of net realized losses at -54m or about 15% of NAV. Leverage remains high at 1.7x - vs. 1.1x for the average BDC.

Although the quarterly performance wasn’t fantastic, it is also not deteriorating, which suggests that the coming reporting quarter could be a decent one for the broader BDC sector.

The Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC) came up on the service. The company went public earlier this year and has been operating since 2021. CEF investors may know the company more as an MLP fund manager with funds like KYN and formerly KMF. The portfolio is lightly leveraged at 0.5x - about half the average and well below the target of 1-1.25x. This makes sense as interest expense is very high at 9.3% vs. the median of around 6.5%.

This is offset somewhat by a low base fee of 0.9% and a lowish 15% incentive fee. The company allocates almost entirely to sponsored deals and first-lien loans. The sector focus is unusual, with not many software companies - typically the top sector in BDCs. The top sectors are trading companies / distributors, food products and commercial services. Non-accruals are low at 1%. The valuation is 97% - just below the median in coverage. Performance has been good, and the NAV is up from the 2021 start. There will be 3 lock-up expires coming up over the next year, which should provide better entry points.

data.title: Comtech's Restructuring Is Risky And Only Makes Sense Under Very Optimistic Assumptions
data.description:
Comtech Telecommunications (NASDAQ:CMTL) is a telecommunications equipment and infrastructure company. The company operates in two segments: satellite and radio frequency equipment (mainly antennas, amplifiers, modems, and troposcatter equipment) and 911 network services (routing, localization processing, VoIP). None of these businesses is particularly well positioned or enjoys good economics.

The company's situation is not great: it has replaced three CEOs in the past two years and spent dozens of millions on severance and restructuring. Its financial condition is difficult, with significant debt paying high interest rates, recently requiring a waiver to avoid default, and low cash reserves.

This week, the company also announced difficulty in filing its FY24 10-K on time, challenges in its satellite business, and the decision to sell its 911 service to pay down debts.

Considering the company's overall management, execution, and financial risks, it does not appear attractive as an investment opportunity, so I consider the name a Hold.

Company info

Satellite and RF business: Comtech derives approximately 70% of its revenues and about half its EBITDA from its Satellite and Space Communications segment. The segment sells components that are used in satellite communication systems, like earth station modems and amplifiers. It also participates in other RF areas, for example, by developing a portable troposcatter system (Comet).

Half of this business' revenues come directly from US government contracts, and I suspect indirectly (via OEMs or foreign governments) the defense industry represents almost all of the segment's revenues.

Contracting for the government carries several disadvantages: the need to invest in designs without winning any bid, a very competitive and technical bidding process, the need to spend on receivables and equipment before being able to bill revenues, etc. Generally, most defense contractors, especially on small scales like Comtech, do not make great returns on capital. For example, the segment's 9M24 adjusted EBITDA margins are 12% (pre-overhead).

Emergency call network business: The second segment is called Terrestrial and Wireless Networks, but basically offers governments and carriers the equipment needed to route, prioritize, locate, and analyze calls to emergency services, mostly 911 but also others like 988 (suicide prevention).

The business reports that most of its sales are not to the US government, but then, by reading the MD&A, one can learn that most customers are local governments, like municipalities. This means the process for gaining business is similarly competitive and uneconomical. Still, the business is a little better, with 15/20% adjusted EBITDA margins (pre-overhead).

Strategy chaos: Comtech has had a few rough years. In 2022 its historical CEO (who had run the company since the 1970s) left. His replacement was the company's COO, part of a transition plan. However, the new CEO lasted 8 months before being removed by the Board. All of this occurred amidst a proxy fight. The second CEO replacement lasted one year and a half, and he was fired in early 2024.

According to the company's latest 10-K, the company had spent almost $40 million on CEO transition costs and the proxy contest before, without considering the 2024 CEO remotion. In addition, the company spent $16 million in restructuring between 2022 and 2023, plus another $9 million in 9M24.

These are the company's direct costs, to which we have to add the enormous but hidden indirect costs of the managerial chaos: not pursuing the correct business, loss of trust with employees, customers and suppliers, etc.

Financially challenged: Comtech's financial position is not good. It has little cash, a lot of debt, and negative cash from operations.

As of 3Q24 (April 2024), and reflecting changes to its finances in June 2024, the company reported $160 million in a term loan yielding SOFR + 10%, and another $25 million in a revolver facility yielding SOFR + 5%. This alone represents $26.5 million in interest a year against $45 million of adjusted EBITDA in 9M24.

In addition, the company has approximately $166 million in preferred shares, yielding 9% payment in kind (6.5% cash). These are not cash payments, but still add $15 million in costs to the company. The shares are convertible at $8 (not in the money currently) and potentially represent a 50% dilution to the company's shareholders.

The company had $30 million in cash and $300 million in receivables and inventories, against the above described debts and $200 million in current liabilities.

The company's CFO cycle is negative ($35 million in 9M24) because it needs to spend a lot on machinery, inventories, etc., before being able to bill to the government, therefore building a lot of receivables (called not unbilled receivables or contract assets). In 9M24, the company's receivables absorbed $45 million.

Strategical review

This week, the company issued a press release with several concerning warnings, plus a key strategic decision.

Inability to file 10-K: The company recognized that it will not be able to file its July 2024 10-K on time.

Challenges in satellite: The company also commented that it 'anticipates reporting significantly lower-than-expected performance, primarily in its S&S segment, in the fourth fiscal quarter.'

Default waiver: Comtech had to receive a waiver to default conditions from its lenders, potentially at the expense of paying even more interest. The waived clause had to do with interest coverage and leverage ratios allowed under the facility.

Decision to sell the emergency business: The company also announced that it is in talks to sell its emergency call network business, for an undisclosed amount. Two of the company's former CEOs had released a letter asking for the sale of this segment. The CEOs mentioned Motorola's acquisition of Rave Mobile for $550 million. In the 4Q22 call, Motorola managers commented that Rave generated $70 million in recurring revenue, meaning the multiple paid was several times revenues. Can the Terrestrial segment be sold for a similar multiple at $160 million in revenues? This is a very optimistic outlook, but it is not easy to confirm. I was not able to find information on Rave's margins, but the price paid implies significant growth, great margins, or a large overvaluation from Motorola.

Valuation

Risk and volatility: Comtech faces enormous execution and financial risks.

On the execution side, the company's businesses are not growing. It has replaced its CEO three times in two years and has spent dozens of millions on restructuring.

On the financial side, the company had to waive a default event, owes a significant amount, and interest eats a big percentage of adjusted EBITDA (almost all of it if adding PIK on preferreds).

This level of risk requires a tremendous level of return potential to be justified. If a normal company with low risk and low growth requires a 12% return (each reader can determine his/her own level of return), then a company like Comtech should provide something like 25% at least.

Post-divestment operating income scenarios: There are two risks in evaluating the divestment of the emergency business. First, we do not know how much of Comtech's overhead is related to the emergency business. Second, we do not know if the proceeds are to be received. The first factor will affect the company's operating income, and the second one is the company's interest expenses and balance sheet. Let's deal with operating income first.

If we observe the segments' profitability for 9M24, we are talking of $45 million in adjusted EBITDA for the two businesses after corporate overhead. The emergency business generated $35 million, and we don't know how much overhead.

If Comtech was unable to reduce overhead after divesting the emergency business, we may be talking about an adjusted EBITDA of $20 million for the after-divestment business. This is quite possible given that companies are generally bad at cutting corporate expenses (especially Comtech, with dozens of millions in CEO termination "non-recurring" expenses). From these $20 million in adjusted EBITDA, we need to remove depreciation of $4 million in the satellite business, R&D special expenses of $4 million, and SBC of $7 million (corporate) to arrive at an operating income of only $1 million. Basically, the satellite segment alone cannot support the current overhead corporate structure.

Suppose half of the overhead goes with the emergency business, using the same calculations. In that case, we can expect about $9/10 million in operating income, not much either, compared with a current market cap of $100 million.

In addition, we have to consider that the company wanted the 4Q24 results for the satellite business to be worse than previously guided.

Proceeds and balance sheet: We don't know how much the emergency business is worth, but we can play with a few assumptions based on EBITDA - CAPEX (adjusted EBITDA - CAPEX, given there's no SBC allocated to the emergency business). For the emergency business, that figure would be around $39 million.

For example, starting with a super optimistic outlook, let's say the company manages to sell the emergency business for 10x EBITDA, or about $390 million. In that case, it can cancel all of its debt and convertible preferred shares and be left with $90 million in extra cash, more than paying for the current market cap ($100 million), and leaving us with a business generating between breakeven and $10 million in operating income. This is not a bad situation.

If we reduce the multiple to 7x EBITDA, which is the average for telecommunication services companies, then we reach $273 million, almost repaying all debts but with no remaining cash. In this case, the valuation does not seem very attractive.

Conclusions

Comtech is a badly managed business with high CEO rotation, much leverage, and high financial risk. The company will divest an essential part of its business under relatively distressed conditions, which may lead to inconvenient sale terms.

It is difficult to evaluate the company's operations, profits, and balance sheet post-divestment, but from the exercises above, it seems that a normal return is only guaranteed under the most optimistic assumptions regarding post-divestment profitability and sale proceeds.

If a very risky investment only generates a regular return under the most optimistic conditions, then it is clearly not an opportunity. For that reason, I believe Comtech is a Hold, but will follow the company's developments closely in the future.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Accelerating Wall Street And Subprime Booms
data.description:
For a quarter when Wall Street trumpeted the narrative of mounting economic weakness and the need for aggressive rate cuts, they sure enjoyed quite an earnings windfall. Is it legitimate (in this day and age) to fret about a downturn and weakening labor markets with Wall Street absolutely booming?

October 16 - Bloomberg (Paige Smith): "Morgan Stanley's record quarter in wealth management capped a banner period for one of Wall Street's most-profitable businesses. Client assets at retail brokerage Charles Schwab Corp. and the wealth arms of the six largest US banks surged $5 trillion in the 12 months through September. That represented a 23% jump as the group's revenue from the business collectively topped $84 billion so far this year. A surge in stock markets has pushed client balances higher and attracted new customers to firms…"

October 16 - Bloomberg (Sridhar Natarajan): "Morgan Stanley traders and bankers joined the rest of their Wall Street rivals in posting better-than-expected revenue, fueling a 32% profit surge for the third quarter and sending the shares up the most in almost four years… The firm's critical wealth unit also stood out, generating record revenue of $7.27 billion, higher than analysts' expectations, with $64 billion in net new assets… 'It was a standout quarter in a constructive environment,' Chief Financial Officer Sharon Yeshaya said... 'We are only getting started and capital markets are only going to get stronger… It's good but it's not the peak.'"

Morgan Stanley reported record third quarter Net Income of $3.377 billion. Total Assets expanded $45.6 billion, or 15% annualized, to a record $1.258 TN. It's worth noting that Total Assets inflated $663 billion, or 111%, since the end of 2019. While on the subject, JPMorgan Total Assets expanded $67 billion during Q3 to a record $4.210 TN, with growth since 2019 at $1.523 TN, or 57%.

October 15 - Bloomberg (Sridhar Natarajan): "Goldman Sachs… profit soared 45% in the third quarter on a surprise increase in equity-trading revenue and a resurgent investment-banking business. The firm's stock traders recorded their best quarter in more than three years, on track for their best year ever, while dealmakers pocketed fees that exceeded estimates across every key business line."

As such a dominant player in securities finance, Goldman Sachs' Q3 data are worthy of a deeper dive. Q3 Earnings surged to $2.78 billion, with y-t-d 2024 earnings 59% higher at $9.602 billion. Investment Banking revenues were up 20% y-o-y (to $1.86bn), Debt Underwriting 46% higher y-o-y ($605 million), and Equity Underwriting up 25% y-o-y ($385 million).

Notably, Net Interest Income surged to $2.62 billion. "3Q24 net interest income increased 70% YoY, reflecting an increase in interest-earning assets - 3Q24 average interest-earning assets of $1.59 trillion." "Global Banking & Markets Net Interest Income up 37% to $1.113bn" - and compares to Q3 '23's $171 million.

With Money Market Fund Assets surging $300 billion during Q3, I've been eagerly waiting for data to confirm a corresponding expansion in "repo" borrowings (funding "basis trade" and other levered speculations).

Goldman's Repo Liability surged $110 billion, or an un-annualized 46%, to a record $348 billion (awaiting Repo Asset detail). Total Assets jumped $74.7 billion, or 18% annualized, to a record $1.728 TN - with one-year growth of $151 billion, or 9.7%. The asset "Short-Term and Long-Term Investments" ballooned $154.6 billion during the quarter (91% annualized) to a record $784 billion, with one-year growth of $239.5 billion, or 44%. Total Assets have inflated $993 billion, or 72%, since the end of 2019.

Goldman's stock surged 6.7% in 10 sessions, boosting y-t-d returns to 39.6%. Stellar Wall Street 2024 returns include JPMorgan's 35.6%, Morgan Stanley's 33.5%, Bank of New York Mellon's 50.6%, and Wells Fargo's 33.5%. The KBW Bank Index sports a y-t-d return of 31.1%, with the Broker/Dealers returning 35.6% (ended Friday at an all-time high).

Highlighting a booming Wall Street, we don't want to ignore the increasingly powerful non-bank players inflating Bubbles in "private Credit" and leveraged lending.

October 17 - Reuters (Echo Wang): "Blackstone beat Wall Street's expectations on its key quarterly earnings metric…, as the world's largest alternative investment firm's assets under management (AUM) hit a record $1.1 trillion... Blackstone said it saw $41 billion of inflows during the third quarter, while it deployed and committed $54 billion of capital - the highest in over two years - amid a revival in dealmaking activity as the U.S. Federal Reserve cut rates and the economic outlook remained sanguine."

October 16 - Bloomberg (Laura Benitez and Jack Sidders): "In its quest to double its size by 2029, Apollo Global Management Inc. is ramping up its ability to write jumbo checks to high-grade corporations as it delves deeper into what it calls private credit's next frontier. The alternative-asset manager is making its high-grade capital-solutions business a key plank in its growth strategy and is beefing up resources for the unit, Apollo Co-President Scott Kleinman said…"

Year-to-date returns include KKR's 69.6%, Blackstone's 34.3%, Apollo Management's 57.5%, and Carlyle Group's 31.7%.

Can we be done with it already? It's illogical to assert that the policy rate is significantly below some hypothetical "neutral rate", with Wall Street enjoying a historic boom.

October 18 - Bloomberg (Olivia Raimonde): "A key measure of perceived riskiness in US blue-chip company bonds has fallen to its lowest levels in two decades, as investors vie for debt that can still offer relatively high yields without too much default risk. The average risk premium, or spread, on a US investment-grade bond compared with comparable Treasuries narrowed to 0.79 percentage point on Thursday, the tightest since 2005…"

As I highlighted in last week's CBB, corporate spreads are today the narrowest since the 2005 Wall Street bonanza. And why wouldn't risk premiums be meager? Risk-taking and speculative leverage are being so handsomely rewarded. Wall Street CEOs are making a fortune with huge compensation packages and stock grants. Traders, investment bankers, loan officers, asset managers, Credit analysts, and derivative players anticipate huge annual bonuses. From CEOs on down, loose finance and powerful asset inflation ensure risk embracement is highly incentivized.

October 15 - Bloomberg (Laura Curtis): "The rise of the private credit market may lead to less systemic risk in the US financial system despite a lack of political appetite for increasing bank capital requirements, Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis President Neel Kashkari said. 'It's scary at some level, because it's exploded to a trillion dollar plus market fairly quickly,' Kashkari said… 'But as I've examined it, a bank in the US today - a big bank - is levered roughly 10 to one, 10 times as much assets for their equity. These private credit vehicles are typically levered one to one, so it's much less leverage… 'So where does systemic risk come from? The intersection between leverage and maturity transformation. So on both those dimensions, these private credit vehicles look like they're much lower risk than banks…"

Mr. Kashkari should know better. The Federal Reserve would be well-advised to reexamine the subprime mortgage Credit fiasco. Few businesses offer the immediate "profit" and growth opportunities available from lending to high-risk borrowers. For one, typically facing limited options, risky borrowers provide a captive audience willing to pay up for money. And once commenced, a "subprime" lending boom is powerfully self-reinforcing.

So long as Credit remains loose (loans readily available), non-performing loans and charge-offs will remain relatively contained. Importantly, this inflates the accounting profitability of risky lending businesses, attracting more opportunistic lenders/speculators, aggressive lending, measly risk premiums, and looser conditions.

Moreover, the rapid growth of risky loan portfolios masks losses from earlier loans turning bad. There's a distinct Ponzi Finance element to "subprime" booms (think 2006-2008 Phoenix housing boom and bust). Eventually, rapidly rising loan losses catch up to slowing portfolio growth, forcing a reassessment of the true risk profile of new loans and the profitability of lending businesses more generally. Loan rates are adjusted higher, while Credit Availability tightens. Losing access to borrowings, tighter conditions hammer risky borrowers that had accumulated (rolled over) excessive debts during the boom.

Whether Mr. Kashkari and his Federal Reserve colleagues recognize it or not, the U.S. economy is in the throes of a historic - and deeply systemic - "subprime" Bubble. So-called "private Credit" is chiefly risky "subprime" corporate lending, having evolved into a primary source of finance for the ongoing proliferation of unprofitable businesses. Ditto the more general "leveraged lending." On the consumer side, "subprime" auto and Credit card lending continues to boom. And I believe we'll look back and view the proliferation of "buy now, pay later" lending programs as principally "subprime."

October 15 - Bloomberg (Laura Curtis): "Klarna Bank AB struck a deal to offload buy-now, pay-later loans that it originates in the UK as it looks for ways to free up capital ahead of its public debut. The deal with a subsidiary of the hedge fund Elliott Investment Management will give Klarna £30 billion ($39bn) of fresh firepower over the coming years as it looks to grow its business around the world… 'By efficiently managing our assets, we can deploy shareholder equity more effectively,' Klarna Chief Financial Officer Niclas Neglen said… 'This is a unique deal, designed to support Klarna's global growth'… The transaction echoes a similar arrangement that KKR & Co. struck with PayPal Holdings Inc. last year to purchase as much as €40 billion ($43bn) of buy-now-pay-later loan receivables…"
